title: Paste paper journal cover
subtitle: Making decorated paste paper and applying it to a journal case
excerpt: Paste paper is made by spreading a coloured wheat-paste or starch-paste over dampened paper and drawing tools through the wet surface to create combed or raked patterns. The technique has been used in European bookbinding for decorated endpapers and covers since the seventeenth century. A finished paste paper sheet is cut to size and glued to a journal case as an outer covering.
sourceNotes: Technique synthesised from Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), public domain; and from general paste-paper practice in the Victorian and Arts and Crafts bookbinding tradition. Cockerell describes paste-paper decorated papers in the context of endpaper and cover-paper production.

glossaryTerms.definition: A decorated paper made by coating a sheet with coloured starch or wheat paste and drawing combs or other tools through the wet surface. Patterns include raked stripes, combed waves, and fingerprinted textures. Used in bookbinding for endpapers and covers since the 17th century.

content.text: produces decoration that is difficult to achieve any other way: the combed or raked paste leaves a texture that is both a surface pattern and a slight physical relief. It is well-suited to journal covers because it is robust once dry, can be cut and folded without cracking, and takes adhesive well. The pattern on each sheet is unique because the paste is moved by hand.

attrs.heading: Safety: cutting tools
attrs.body: Use a metal ruler when trimming the paste paper cover to size. Keep fingers behind the ruler edge. Change the blade when it starts to drag rather than cut cleanly; a blunt blade requires more force and is more likely to slip.

items.name: Cartridge paper, 120-160 gsm
items.description: Heavy enough to hold the paste without excessive buckling when wet. The finished sheet will be slightly cockled: this flattens out under pressure after drying.

items.name: Wheat flour or cornflour paste
items.description: Mix 1 tablespoon of flour with cold water to a smooth thin paste, then add 250 ml of boiling water and stir over heat until the mixture thickens and clears. Allow to cool before adding colour. Wallpaper paste (methyl cellulose) is a convenient alternative that does not require cooking.

items.name: Acrylic paint or gouache
items.description: Added to the cooled paste. Start with 2-3 teaspoons of paint per cup of paste; adjust to taste. Multiple colours can be applied side by side on the sheet before combing.

items.name: Paste brush (slug: paste-brush-bookbinding)
items.description: For spreading paste evenly over the dampened paper surface. A stiff-bristled brush covers the sheet faster than a soft one.

items.name: Combing tools
items.description: A wide-tooth comb, a fork, a card cut with notches, or a crumpled cloth. Each leaves a different mark in the paste.

content.text: Before applying paste, dampen the paper sheet on both sides with a sponge or damp cloth. Lay it on a piece of board or a plastic sheet. Dampening prevents the dry paper absorbing the paste unevenly and reduces buckling during the drying process. The paper should be uniformly damp: not soaking.

Spread the coloured paste over the dampened sheet in an even coat using the paste brush: work quickly so the paste does not begin to set before you start patterning. Draw the combing tool through the paste in continuous strokes. Parallel stripes require a single steady direction; waved or chevron patterns come from moving the comb laterally while advancing it. Work in one pass where possible; re-entering an area after the paste has started to skin leaves visible seams in the pattern.

Lift the patterned sheet by one corner and hang it to air dry, or lay it flat and allow it to dry naturally. The sheet will cockle as the paste shrinks. Once fully dry, place it face-down between two sheets of clean cartridge paper and press under a flat board with a heavy weight for several hours. This flattens the sheet and leaves the paste surface undisturbed.

Cut the paste paper to the cover dimensions with a craft knife and metal ruler, adding a 15-20 mm turnover allowance on all four edges. Apply a thin, even coat of PVA glue to the board (not to the paste paper) with the paste brush. Position the board on the back of the paste paper, press and smooth from the centre outward using the bone folder. Mitre the corners, fold the turnovers in, and burnish flat. Place under weight until dry. The paste paper cover is robust enough for everyday journal use.
